data.title: Market Voices: Goolsbee on Fed rates, Buffet-UnitedHealth, Corning-T1
data.description:
Seeking Alpha's roundup of statements, announcements and remarks that could impact markets, sectors or individual stocks.

  • Chicago Federal Reserve President Austan Goolsbee said he wants to see more inflation data before he decides whether to vote to lower interest rates when the Federal Open Market Committee meets in September.

“It’s been a little mixed,” Goolsbee told CNBC on Friday, referring to the most recent inflation data reports. “I feel like we still need another one, at least, to figure out if we’re if we’re still on the golden path.”

“It all depends on the data and what’s the economic outlook. If we keep getting inflation reports like [previous] ones ... I would be very comfortable that, hey, the dust is out of the air, it looks like we’re still where we were, which is a strong economy with inflation coming back down,” Goolsbee added.

  • Warren Buffet's Berkshire Hathaway (NYSE:BRK.A) (NYSE:BRK.B) has taken a significant stake in troubled healthcare company UnitedHealth Group (NYSE:UNH).

Berkshire Hathaway said in an SEC filing that it now holds approximately 5 million shares of UnitedHealth, valued at around $1.57 billion as of June 30.

  • Corning (NYSE:GLW) and T1 Energy (NYSE:TE) are teaming up to establish a fully domestic supply chain for the production of solar panel components, which has been dominated by China. 

Under the deal, Corning will supply hyper-pure polysilicon and solar wafers made in its Michigan plant to T1 for the production of solar modules at T1's Texas facility.  

“This landmark supply chain agreement with Corning will help invigorate America with scalable, reliable, low-cost energy,” said T1 Chairman and CEO Daniel Barcelo, in a statement. “This is American companies building in America and protecting American energy security."

“Corning is proud to help meet the growing need for solar products made in the United States,” said AB Ghosh, chairman and CEO of Corning's Hemlock Semiconductor unit, in the same statement. “We’re accelerating the ramp of our advanced manufacturing capabilities to support a resilient U.S. solar supply chain."

data.title: Par Technology rises after rating upgrade at BTIG
data.description:
Shares of Par Technology (NYSE:PAR) rose about 2% on Friday after BTIG upgraded the stock to Buy from Neutral, noting that the company's pipeline will comfortably drive over three years of more than 15% growth. 

The firm has set a $65 price target on the stock.

Analysts led by Andrew Harte believe that Par's $100M sales pipeline will enable the business to comfortably drive more than 15% growth for the next several years. On top of that growth, Par is competing for three "megadeals" with top-20 restaurant brands, which are not included in the $100M sales pipeline and could help drive growth to over 20%.

The analysts think Par is proving to be the premier enterprise restaurant tech solution, similar to what Toast (TOST) is for small and medium-sized businesses, or SMBs, and as such deserves a premium multiple.

Par's stock has traded about 25% lower over the last month, first from investors thinking it may be stretching to acquire Olo (OLO) (which management denied), and then more recently from disappointing second quarter 2025 results and a reduced growth outlook to mid-teens, according to the analysts.

"We see the stock's recent pullback as an attractive entry point and think growth is more likely to exceed expectations than fall short during the next year," said  Harte and his team.

data.title: HHS reinstates childhood vaccines task force
data.description:
An HHS task force responsible for oversight of childhood shots that was disbanded in 1998 is coming back.

The Task Force on Safer Childhood Vaccines is responsible for childhood vaccine safety oversight. The panel was created by Congress.

It will be headed by National Institutes of Health Director Jay Bhattacharya.

The panel will work with the Advisory Commission on Childhood Vaccines to develop recommendations on the development of childhood vaccines that have fewer and less serious side effects than those presently on the market as well as boost adverse reaction reporting. 

The task force's first report to Congress is due in two years, with subsequent reports every two years.

"I fear we are going to be right back to where we were in the 1980s, when the making of these vaccines became more and more onerous for the companies who make them," Paul Offit, director of the Vaccine Education Center at the Children's Hospital of Philadelphia, told Axios. "Vaccines are going to be less available, less affordable, and there is going to be more fear."

In May, Children's Health Defense, an anti-vaccine organization previously chaired by HHS Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr., sued him in that capacity to revive the task force. 

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: ICU Medical wins bullish view at Piper Sandler on upcoming product cycle wave
data.description:
Shares of ICU Medical (NASDAQ:ICUI) spiked on Friday after Piper Sandler initiated its coverage with an Overweight rating and a $145 price target, citing an imminent new product cycle wave for the infusion pump maker.

Analyst Jason Bednar argued that California-based ICU Medical (NASDAQ:ICUI) is well-positioned to capitalize on a “large replacement opportunity” in the infusion pump market, which he described as having plenty of tailwinds, including strict regulatory barriers and lasting mid-single-digit growth.

Noting the company’s recently approved infusion pumps, Plum Solo and Plum Duo, the analyst wrote: “For these pumps, and considering the RFP/sales discussion timeline, management has referenced the upgrade cycles as commencing later this year and into next year.” “And we anticipate the launch of Solo and Duo could have a nice multi-year tail given the replacement needs across the market,” he added.

Bednar also noted that ICU Medical’s (NASDAQ:ICUI) recently submitted regulatory filings for the CADD ambulatory pump and Medfusion syringe pump give it “a host of new pump launches that can help carry revenue growth for the business over at least the next few years.”

Given potential improvements for ICUI’s gross margin and free cash flow, the analyst argues that the stock deserves an NTM P/E multiple of at least the upper teens compared to the five- and 10-year averages of 23x and 26x, respectively.

data.title: Celcuity targets $5B addressable market as gedatolisib posts unprecedented PFS gains and patent extends to 2042
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Celcuity Inc. (CELC) Q2 2025

Management View

  • Brian F. Sullivan, CEO, announced several milestones, including positive top line data from the PIK3CA wild-type cohort of the Phase III VIKTORIA-1 trial, initiation of dosing in the VIKTORIA-2 trial, encouraging results from two early-phase studies, and extension of patent exclusivity for gedatolisib into 2042.

  • Sullivan emphasized, "The first and most importantly, of course, was the positive top line data we reported from the PIK3CA wild-type cohort of our Phase III VIKTORIA-1 clinical trial...The reported hazard ratios and improvements in median PFS are unprecedented in HR-positive, HER2-negative advanced breast cancer."

  • The CEO highlighted that the company raised approximately $287 million through public offerings, positioning Celcuity to prepare for a potential launch of gedatolisib upon FDA approval next year.

  • Vicky Hahne, CFO, stated, "Our second quarter net loss was $45.3 million or $1.04 per share compared to $23.7 million net loss or $0.62 per share for the second quarter of 2024...We ended the quarter with approximately $168.4 million of cash, cash equivalents and short-term investments. However, on a pro forma basis...cash, cash equivalents and short-term investments as of the end of Q2 2025 was approximately $455 million."

Outlook

  • The company plans to submit a new drug application to the FDA in Q4 2025 for gedatolisib based on data from the PIK3CA wild-type cohort.

  • Full data from this cohort will be presented later in the year, with top line data from the PIK3CA mutation cohort expected by year-end 2025.

  • Management projects the addressable market potential for a standard of care second-line therapy in this patient population is roughly $5 billion.

Financial Results

  • The second quarter net loss was $45.3 million or $1.04 per share, and the non-GAAP adjusted net loss was $40.5 million or $0.93 per share.

  • Research and development expenses rose to $40.2 million, with a $17.7 million increase driven by higher employee and consulting expenses, increased R&D costs, and an anticipated $5 million milestone payment to Pfizer.

  • General and administrative expenses increased to $3.8 million, attributed mostly to employee and consulting costs.

  • Net cash used in operating activities was $36.2 million, ending the quarter with $168.4 million in cash. Including Q3 financing, cash and equivalents were $455 million, and management expects access to an additional $160 million through existing arrangements.

Q&A

  • Mohamad Amin Makarem, Jefferies: Asked about subgroup analysis in the upcoming full data presentation and benchmarks for PIK3CA mutant success. Sullivan responded that initial presentations will focus on primary analyses, with subgroup data at later meetings. For the mutant cohort, Sullivan said, "If we have a positive study, we'll also be reporting clinically meaningful results."

  • Frances Dovell, TD Cowen: Inquired about detailed safety data. Sullivan replied, "We were really only at this stage able to provide a general summary of what we saw, but the additional detail will be forthcoming."

  • Eason Lee, Leerink Partners: Asked about PFS analysis methods and regulatory expectations. Sullivan explained the use of BICR due to the open-label structure and noted, "All the sensitivity analyses are indicating that our data is very robust, and we're very comfortable and confident about the package that we expect to submit to the FDA."

  • Stephen Douglas Willey, Stifel: Questioned launch-readiness and infrastructure. Sullivan outlined that the commercial team build-out began last year, and expansion is ongoing in preparation for launch, emphasizing the experience of new hires.

  • Gil Joseph Blum, Needham & Company: Asked about physician options with both doublet and triplet regimens. Sullivan indicated that both regimens allow broader patient access and that future subgroup results will guide clinical decisions.

  • Chase Richard Knickerbocker, Craig-Hallum: Probed competitive landscape and manufacturing. Sullivan addressed competition and highlighted confidence in the CMC package, stating, "We're very confident just based on the robustness of the package that we've built and the data that we've generated that we should satisfy the FDA's requirements."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts asked detailed questions about clinical data presentation, regulatory expectations, safety, and commercial infrastructure, showing an inquisitive but neutral to slightly positive tone.

  • Management displayed confidence and clarity in both prepared remarks and responses, using strong language such as "we are very comfortable and confident about the package that we expect to submit to the FDA."

  • Compared to the previous quarter, analysts maintained a similar tone, while management's confidence increased due to positive trial data and strengthened financial position.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter featured the announcement of unprecedented PFS gains and hazard ratios for gedatolisib, while the prior quarter focused on upcoming data readouts and operational progress.

  • Guidance language shifted from anticipation to specific plans for regulatory submission and commercial launch preparation.

  • Analysts' questions moved from trial timelines and data expectations to practical details of product launch, market positioning, and competitive differentiation.

  • Management's tone became more assertive, supported by new clinical and financial milestones.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged the need for detailed subgroup and safety data, with plans to release more information at conferences later in the year.

  • Analysts raised questions about competitive landscape, infrastructure investment, and benchmarks for clinical success.

  • Management emphasized a strong balance sheet and robust planning for commercial rollout as mitigation strategies.

Final Takeaway

Celcuity highlighted multiple significant milestones this quarter, including positive Phase III trial outcomes for gedatolisib, issuance of a new dosing regimen patent extending exclusivity to 2042, and a strengthened balance sheet with over $450 million pro forma cash. Management is preparing for a Q4 2025 FDA submission, and the addressable market for gedatolisib is projected at $5 billion. With robust clinical data, favorable safety profile, and broad launch preparations underway, the company is positioned to advance gedatolisib as a new standard of care for a large, underserved breast cancer population.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Galaxy Digital secures $1.4B financing to expand Helios AI data center
data.description:
Galaxy Digital Holdings (NASDAQ:GLXY) (OTCPK:BRPHF) has closed a $1.4B project financing facility to further expand its Helios data center campus in West Texas, it said on Friday.

The debt facility will fully fund the initial retrofit and expansion of Helios to deliver the first phase of power for AI and high-performance computing ("HPC") operations under a long-term agreement with CoreWeave (CRWV).

Galaxy (NASDAQ:GLXY) has already provided the $350M equity requirement, with the remaining construction costs to be covered by the debt facility. The $1.4B financing, which was secured at 80% loan to cost, has a 36-month term and is secured by all the assets associated with the first phase of Helios's buildout.

"This financing marks a major milestone in our transformation of Helios into a next-generation AI and HPC datacenter campus," said Mike Novogratz, Founder and CEO of Galaxy. "We're on track and excited to deliver the first phase of power to CoreWeave (CRWV) beginning in early 2026. This project is a key step in diversifying Galaxy's business model as we expand beyond crypto and into the broader AI infrastructure space."

Earlier in the month, Galaxy struck the lease deal with CoreWeave (CRWV) for the second phase of the Helios development. CoreWeave has now committed to the full 800 megawatts of approved power capacity at Helios. 

Galaxy (GLXY) said it expects to generate average annual revenue of over $1B over the 15-year term of the agreements with CoreWeave (CRWV), citing committed contractual terms, internal estimates for capital expenditures and the assumption of full capacity utilization of critical IT load it has agreed to provide.

BRPHF shares retreated 7.5% in late morning trading.

data.title: Short interest ticks up in S&P 500 Materials sector; Albemarle tops the list
data.description:
Short interest in materials stocks inched higher in July compared to late June, with Albemarle (ALB) emerging as the sector’s most shorted stock. The increase comes amid heightened geopolitical tensions, which continue to drive market volatility and reshape global trade dynamics.

Average short interest across materials stocks in the S&P 500 index rose to 2.92% of shares floating at the end of July from 2.85% a month ago.

The S&P 500 Materials sector (NYSEARCA:XLB) has gained 7% year-to-date, underperforming the broader S&P 500 (SP500), which is up about 10%.

Stocks with the largest and least short positions

Ranked by short interest as a percentage of shares float

 

 

Seeking Alpha

Lithium producer Albemarle (ALB) continues to be the most shorted stock in the materials sector, with 14.91 million shares sold short as of July 31—representing 12.67% of its float, down slightly from 14.79% at the end of June.

Albemarle aims for positive free cash flow in 2025 as cost actions strengthen financial flexibility.  The company’s COO, Netha Johnson, will be succeeded by Mark Mummert, among various changes to the organizational structure, it said will "further align the company for agility and efficiency."

International Paper (IP) was the second most shorted materials stock at 8.63% of the shares float. The pulp and paper company slumped after falling short of profit estimates with its second-quarter earnings report.

Meanwhile, short interests against CF Industries Holdings (CF) climbed up and stood at 5.29% from 4.95% a month earlier.

Barclays analyst Benjamin Theurer on Wednesday upgraded CF Industries (NYSE:CF) to Overweight from Equal Weight and cited favourable market fundamentals, the start-up of low-carbon ammonia production, and continued share buybacks as key drivers.

Agricultural sciences company Corteva (CTVA), on the other hand, took the spot for the least shorted stock in the pack and was preceded by Ecolab (ECL) and DuPont (DD).

According to one Seeking Alpha author, despite pricing headwinds and manageable tariff risks, CTVA’s share repurchase plan and operational improvements underpin a positive outlook for the stock.

 

Industry Analysis:

 

 

Seeking Alpha

Containers and packaging remain the most shorted industry in the sector, with short interest at 3.79% as of the end of July, up from 3.67% a month earlier.

Construction materials took the second spot, with short interest easing to 3.07% at the end of July, from 2.72% the previous month.

data.title: SWK Holdings signals ongoing capital returns and streamlined focus following major asset sales
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: SWK Holdings Corporation (SWKH) Q2 2025

Management View

  • Jody D. Staggs, President and CEO, stated that during the second quarter, "we completed a sale of the majority of our royalty assets, and after quarter close, we completed the sale of the majority of the assets at our MOD3 subsidiary." Staggs highlighted that the sale was executed for approximately book value, which was a premium to where SWK has historically traded.
  • Staggs emphasized the return of $49 million to shareholders through a $4 per share dividend and an additional $3 million year-to-date through the repurchase of approximately 200,000 shares. Staggs noted, "We believe these steps demonstrate the organization's focus on realizing the underlying value of our assets and ensuring that shareholders benefit from these realizations."
  • Staggs described the current asset structure as "cash, $234 million of gross performing first lien term loans with an effective yield of 14.1% and of gross nonperforming reorg royalties, $5 million of public equities warrants and approximately 11 private warrants and earnouts, which are carried at $0 for GAAP purposes." An $8.8 million general loan loss reserve was also mentioned.
  • Staggs reported, "For the second quarter, both our non-GAAP adjusted net income and finance segment adjusted non-GAAP net income totaled $4.6 million. We believe this level is a reasonable run rate for the business going forward."
  • Adam Christopher Rice, CFO, stated, "We reported GAAP pretax net income of $4.6 million or $0.37 per diluted share. Our reported second quarter 2020 net income to $3.5 million after income tax expense of just over $1 million."

Outlook

  • Staggs stated, "We believe the second quarter's results are a reasonable proxy for the earnings power of the business going forward."
  • Staggs detailed that the business simplification and asset sales position SWK for continued focus on shareholder returns and operational efficiency.

Financial Results

  • Rice reported, "This includes a $1.2 million decrease in year-over-year finance receivables segment revenue and $500,000 increase in year-over-year Pharmaceutical Development segment revenue. The $1.2 million decrease in year-over-year financing receivables segment revenue was primarily due to a $3.4 million decrease in interest and fees due to paydowns, payoffs and the sale of the majority of our royalty portfolio."
  • Operating expenses for the quarter were $5.4 million. MOD3 operating expenses decreased to $1.2 million compared to $2.5 million in the prior year period, and finance receivables segment operating expenses were $4.2 million versus $7.4 million a year earlier. Rice attributed the decrease to a $3.3 million reduction in provision for credit losses.
  • Rice noted, "As of June 30, 2025, our GAAP book value per share was $20.23 an 11% decrease compared to $22.72 as of June 30, 2024. Adjusting for the $4 per share dividend paid during the quarter, the GAAP book value per share was $24.46 and a 6.8% increase year-over-year."
  • The company bought back just under 60,000 shares for $900,000 during the quarter and repurchased an additional 8,000 shares after quarter close for $1.3 million in total.

Q&A

  • Scott Jensen, Private Investor: "With the MOD3 sale, obviously, we'll see a bump in revenue in the third quarter. But what about the costs associated with that business going on to Aptar, do you have any recurring costs that remain on your side of the ledger. And then kind of what would that maybe SG&A impact the now that Aptar owns it?" Staggs responded, "Third quarter will be a little bit messy because we did agree to a transition services agreement, which runs through mid-September. Now we are getting those costs reimbursed. So all the cost of the business went to Aptar close. There's no ongoing cost at MOD3."
  • Jensen asked about regulatory changes, specifically at the FDA, and their impact on the portfolio. Staggs replied, "Near term, I get the current thought is that there could be fewer drugs approved. That doesn't really impact our portfolio. We don't have any drug companies or device companies that are pending some type of approved products. So not a big concern there."
  • Jensen inquired about the impact of increased private credit participation. Staggs answered, "We've been fairly tempered on the deployment side, we've been able to add some on to existing performing borrowers, which is always great. We made one new loan to an Australian company that was a little bit off the run and we felt good fine about that on kind of a core deal for us. So we've been pretty disciplined."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a neutral and constructive tone, focusing on cost impacts of the MOD3 sale, regulatory risks, and the competitive landscape in private credit. Questions were direct and sought clarity on cost structure and risk exposure.
  • Management's tone was confident during prepared remarks, emphasizing asset sales and returns to shareholders, but became slightly cautious when discussing transition costs and industry competition, using phrases such as "a little bit messy" and "we've been pretty disciplined."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, the sentiment remains confident regarding asset value realization, with a slight increase in caution due to ongoing industry changes and capital deployment challenges.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter featured a significant strategic shift with the sale of the majority of royalty assets and MOD3 subsidiary assets, compared to the previous quarter's focus on portfolio growth and ongoing MOD3 partnership.
  • Guidance language shifted from outlining growth opportunities to describing a "reasonable run rate" for future earnings, reflecting a more stable, post-asset-sale business profile.
  • Operating expenses decreased quarter-over-quarter, and management highlighted continued capital returns to shareholders.
  • Analyst focus evolved from asset value and credit quality in Q1 to operational impacts and competitive risks in Q2.
  • Management's confidence in asset realization remains strong, but there is increased emphasis on maintaining discipline in new deployments amid heightened market competition.

Risks and Concerns

  • Staggs identified regulatory risks, including potential FDA changes and scientific funding cuts, but indicated no material impact on the current portfolio.
  • Concerns about pricing risk were addressed, with Staggs noting that the portfolio's specialty pharma exposure and product mix mitigate risk.
  • The impact of increased competition in private credit was acknowledged, with management stating the need for caution and disciplined deployment.

Final Takeaway

SWK Holdings closed a transformative quarter, marked by major asset sales and significant capital returns to shareholders. The company now presents a simplified balance sheet, strong liquidity, and a commitment to disciplined deployment. Management views the second quarter's results as a reliable indicator for future earnings and remains focused on shareholder value, operational efficiency, and prudent risk management as the company navigates an evolving lending landscape.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Rivian claims $100M in CAFE credit sales stalled by policy reversal - report
data.description:
Rivian Automotive (NASDAQ:RIVN) says the U.S. government has tied up $100M in revenue from the sale of regulatory credits to legacy automakers as part of Washington’s push to reverse the prior administration’s fuel economy policies.

According to The Wall Street Journal, Rivian (NASDAQ:RIVN) and other EV automakers generated hundreds of millions of dollars in revenue selling regulatory credits to competitors whose vehicles exceed Corporate Average Fuel Economy, or CAFE, standards. However, Washington’s decision to remove penalties for violating CAFE standards led the National Highway Traffic Safety Administration (NHTSA) to delay issuing annual CAFE compliance notifications as it reconsiders standards for cars made in 2022 and after.

Accordingly, EV automakers have not received compensation for selling credits. For smaller EV makers like Rivian (NASDAQ:RIVN) and Lucid (LCID), the regulatory credits comprise a meaningful amount of revenue. Rivian estimates regulatory credits made up 6.5% of total revenue for the first half of 2025.

To address the Administration’s decision to suspend CAFE standards, the Zero Emission Transportation Association (ZETA) filed a petition with the Washington, D.C. U.S. Court of Appeals to force NHTSA to resume issuing CAFE compliance letters. NHTSA, however, has said it will return to its prior policy of issuing compliance letters once the agency has “reconsidered CAFE standards to make cars more affordable again.”

In the meantime, Rivian (RIVN) says it is out $100M in regulatory credits it has already negotiated for but can’t finalize, while Tesla (TSLA) claims the recent regulatory actions have resulted in a $1.1B revenue shortfall from selling credits. At the same time, General Motors (GM) is said to have spent $3.5B since 2022 to purchase CAFE credits.
